revision.comment: Created by translating the page "[[:ja:Special:Redirect/revision/89770415|神奈備]]"
revision.text:
[[File:Miwayama201805.jpg|thumb|300x300px|[[Mount Miwa|三輪山]]

[[Yamato Province|大和国]][[Ichinomiya|一宮]]・[[Ōmiwa Shrine|大神神社]]の神奈備（[[Sacred mountains|神体山]]）。大神神社は山を御神体とし、[[Shinto architecture|本殿]]がない。]] '''神奈備'''（かむなび・かんなび・かみなび）とは、[[Shinto|神道]]において、神霊（[[Kami|神]]や[[Mitama|御霊]]）が宿る御霊代（みたましろ）・[[Yorishiro|依り代]]（よりしろ）を擁した領域のこと。または、神代（かみしろ）として自然環境を[[Shintai|神体]]（しんたい）とすること。万葉集においての表記は7つ(22首、23例)ある。 神が「鎮座する」または「隠れ住まう」山や森の神域や、[[Himorogi|神籬]]（ひもろぎ）・[[Iwakura (Yorishiro)|磐座]]（いわくら）となる森林や[[Shinboku|神木]]（しんぼく）や鎮守の森や[[Sacred mountains|神体山]][[富士山|霊峰富士]]など。[[山岳信仰]]を参照を、また特徴的な岩（{{ill|Meotoiwa|ja|夫婦岩}}）や滝（[[Nachi Falls|那智滝]]）がある神域などをさす。神籬と磐座の総称でもある。依り代となる森林や岩などがない「神奈備野」もある。 == 概要 == 「カンナビ」は「神並び」の「カンナラビ」が「カンナビ」となったとする説や、「ナビ」は「隠れる」を意味し「神が隠れ籠れる」場所とする説がある。もっとも後者は、上代特殊仮名遣いの発見により否定されている。 神奈備は[[Animism|アニミズム]]でもあり、自然への感謝や畏敬や畏怖の体現であるが、神の住まう{{Ill|Shin'iki|it}}や、{{ill|Eternal world|ja|常世}}（とこよ）と{{ill|This world|ja|現世|de|Diesseits|bs|Dunjaluk}}（うつしよ）の端境、または、その常世と現世をわかつ[[Bai sema|結界]]や、禁足地なども意味する。 自然を手付かずに残す事例として、[[Natural environment|自然環境]]の[[Environmental movement|保護]]の観点からも重視され、里山やその周囲の文化として貴重であり、固有の土壌細菌の発見が新薬の開発のきっかけとなることがあるほか、世界中の自然環境学の研究者などが、研究に訪れる場所でもある。 現在の[[Shrine Shinto|神社神道]]の神体は「社（やしろ）」であり、神奈備とはいわない。神社神道も本来は日本で自然発生的に生まれた[[Prehistoric religion|原始宗教]]といわれ、[[Nature worship|自然崇拝]]や[[Animism|精霊崇拝]]を内包する[[Ko-Shintō|古神道]]から派生して現在に至る。現在の[[Shinto shrine|神社]]には、主たる{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神}}の[[Kami|尊]]（みこと）とは別に、「自然」という神体が存在するのが常で、神体として[[Shimenawa|注連縄]]が飾られた社とともに、{{ill|Keidai|ja|境内}}の内外に神木や霊石や鎮守の森の湖沼や滝などの神体が存在する。古い神社では、[[Shinto architecture|拝殿]]や[[Shinto architecture|本殿]]もなく、自然の神奈備そのものを祭神として[[Festival|祀]]るところもある。 == 『出雲国風土記』におけるカンナビ山 == [[File:Mount_Obune,_enkei-1.jpg|right|thumb|250x250px|Jinnai Hiyama (Mt. Ofunayama), Tatemae-gun]] 『{{Ill|Izumo Fudoki|de}}』には「カンナビ」の山が4か所記載される。「カンナビ」の漢字表記はさまざま。 ; 意宇郡の神名樋野 : [[Matsue|松江市]]の[[Chausuyama|茶臼山]]に比定。 ; 秋鹿郡の神名火山 : 通説では松江市の[[Asahiyama|朝日山]]に比定される。 ; 楯縫郡の神名樋山 : [[Izumo, Shimane|出雲市]]の大船山に比定。 ; 出雲郡の神名火山 : 出雲市の仏経山に比定。 == 脚注 == == 関連事項 == * [[Age of the Gods|神代]]・上代 * [[Ko-Shintō|古神道]] * [[Himorogi|神籬]]・[[Iwakura (Yorishiro)|磐座]] * [[Shintai|神体]]・[[Yorishiro|依り代]] * 鎮守の森・[[Shinboku|神木]] * [[Sacred mountains|神体山]]・[[Mountain worship|山岳信仰]] * [[Kannagi (Shinto)|かんなぎ]] * 神道の緑性 * 三輪山 [[Category:Forests of Japan]] [[Category:Animism]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

[[File:Miwayama201805.jpg|thumb|300x300px|[[Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]]

Kannabi ([[Sacred mountains|Kintai]]) in [[Yamato Province|Yamato Province][[Ichinomiya|Ichinomiya]] and [[Ōmiwa Shrine|Okami Shrine]]. Ookami Shrine has a mountain as its sacred body and does not have a [[Shinto architecture|honden]].]] '''神奈備'''（かむなび・かんなび・かみなび）とは、[[Shinto|神道]]において、神霊（[[Kami|神]]や[[Mitama|御霊]]）が宿る御霊代（みたましろ）・[[Yorishiro|依り代]]（よりしろ）を擁した領域のこと。または、神代（かみしろ）として自然環境を[[Shintai|神体]]（しんたい）とすること。万葉集においての表記は7つ(22首、23例)ある。 神が「鎮座する」または「隠れ住まう」山や森の神域や、[[Himorogi|神籬]]（ひもろぎ）・[[Iwakura (Yorishiro)|磐座]]（いわくら）となる森林や[[Shinboku|神木]]（しんぼく）や鎮守の森や[[Sacred mountains|神体山]][[富士山|霊峰富士]]など。[[山岳信仰]]を参照を、また特徴的な岩（{{ill|Meotoiwa|ja|夫婦岩}}）や滝（[[Nachi Falls|那智滝]]）がある神域などをさす。神籬と磐座の総称でもある。依り代となる森林や岩などがない「神奈備野」もある。 == 概要 == 「カンナビ」は「神並び」の「カンナラビ」が「カンナビ」となったとする説や、「ナビ」は「隠れる」を意味し「神が隠れ籠れる」場所とする説がある。もっとも後者は、上代特殊仮名遣いの発見により否定されている。 神奈備は[[Animism|アニミズム]]でもあり、自然への感謝や畏敬や畏怖の体現であるが、神の住まう{{Ill|Shin'iki|it}}や、{{ill|Eternal world|ja|常世}}（とこよ）と{{ill|This world|ja|現世|de|Diesseits|bs|Dunjaluk}}（うつしよ）の端境、または、その常世と現世をわかつ[[Bai sema|結界]]や、禁足地なども意味する。 自然を手付かずに残す事例として、[[Natural environment|自然環境]]の[[Environmental movement|保護]]の観点からも重視され、里山やその周囲の文化として貴重であり、固有の土壌細菌の発見が新薬の開発のきっかけとなることがあるほか、世界中の自然環境学の研究者などが、研究に訪れる場所でもある。 現在の[[Shrine Shinto|神社神道]]の神体は「社（やしろ）」であり、神奈備とはいわない。神社神道も本来は日本で自然発生的に生まれた[[Prehistoric religion|原始宗教]]といわれ、[[Nature worship|自然崇拝]]や[[Animism|精霊崇拝]]を内包する[[Ko-Shintō|古神道]]から派生して現在に至る。現在の[[Shinto shrine|神社]]には、主たる{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神}}の[[Kami|尊]]（みこと）とは別に、「自然」という神体が存在するのが常で、神体として[[Shimenawa|注連縄]]が飾られた社とともに、{{ill|Keidai|ja|境内}}の内外に神木や霊石や鎮守の森の湖沼や滝などの神体が存在する。古い神社では、[[Shinto architecture|拝殿]]や[[Shinto architecture|本殿]]もなく、自然の神奈備そのものを祭神として[[Festival|祀]]るところもある。 == 『出雲国風土記』におけるカンナビ山 == [[File:Mount_Obune,_enkei-1.jpg|right|thumb|250x250px|Jinnai Hiyama (Mt. Ofunayama), Tatemae-gun]] 『{{Ill|Izumo Fudoki|de}}』には「カンナビ」の山が4か所記載される。「カンナビ」の漢字表記はさまざま。 ; 意宇郡の神名樋野 : [[Matsue|松江市]]の[[Chausuyama|茶臼山]]に比定。 ; 秋鹿郡の神名火山 : 通説では松江市の[[Asahiyama|朝日山]]に比定される。 ; 楯縫郡の神名樋山 : [[Izumo, Shimane|出雲市]]の大船山に比定。 ; 出雲郡の神名火山 : 出雲市の仏経山に比定。 == 脚注 == == 関連事項 == * [[Age of the Gods|神代]]・上代 * [[Ko-Shintō|古神道]] * [[Himorogi|神籬]]・[[Iwakura (Yorishiro)|磐座]] * [[Shintai|神体]]・[[Yorishiro|依り代]] * 鎮守の森・[[Shinboku|神木]] * [[Sacred mountains|神体山]]・[[Mountain worship|山岳信仰]] * [[Kannagi (Shinto)|かんなぎ]] * 神道の緑性 * 三輪山 [[Category:Forests of Japan]] [[Category:Animism]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

[[File:Miwayama201805.jpg|thumb|300x300px|[[Mount Miwa|三輪山]]

[[File:Miwayama201805.jpg|thumb|300x300px|[[Mount Miwa|三輪山]]

[[Yamato Province|Yamato Country]] [[Ichinomiya|Ichinomiya]], [[Ōmiwa Shrine|Okami Shrine]], Kannabi ([[Sacred mountains|Kintai-san]]). Ookami Shrine has a mountain as its sacred body and does not have a [[Shinto architecture|honden]].]] '''神奈備'''（かむなび・かんなび・かみなび）とは、[[Shinto|神道]]において、神霊（[[Kami|神]]や[[Mitama|御霊]]）が宿る御霊代（みたましろ）・[[Yorishiro|依り代]]（よりしろ）を擁した領域のこと。または、神代（かみしろ）として自然環境を[[Shintai|神体]]（しんたい）とすること。万葉集においての表記は7つ(22首、23例)ある。 神が「鎮座する」または「隠れ住まう」山や森の神域や、[[Himorogi|神籬]]（ひもろぎ）・[[Iwakura (Yorishiro)|磐座]]（いわくら）となる森林や[[Shinboku|神木]]（しんぼく）や鎮守の森や[[Sacred mountains|神体山]][[富士山|霊峰富士]]など。[[山岳信仰]]を参照を、また特徴的な岩（{{ill|Meotoiwa|ja|夫婦岩}}）や滝（[[Nachi Falls|那智滝]]）がある神域などをさす。神籬と磐座の総称でもある。依り代となる森林や岩などがない「神奈備野」もある。 == 概要 == 「カンナビ」は「神並び」の「カンナラビ」が「カンナビ」となったとする説や、「ナビ」は「隠れる」を意味し「神が隠れ籠れる」場所とする説がある。もっとも後者は、上代特殊仮名遣いの発見により否定されている。 神奈備は[[Animism|アニミズム]]でもあり、自然への感謝や畏敬や畏怖の体現であるが、神の住まう{{Ill|Shin'iki|it}}や、{{ill|Eternal world|ja|常世}}（とこよ）と{{ill|This world|ja|現世|de|Diesseits|bs|Dunjaluk}}（うつしよ）の端境、または、その常世と現世をわかつ[[Bai sema|結界]]や、禁足地なども意味する。 自然を手付かずに残す事例として、[[Natural environment|自然環境]]の[[Environmental movement|保護]]の観点からも重視され、里山やその周囲の文化として貴重であり、固有の土壌細菌の発見が新薬の開発のきっかけとなることがあるほか、世界中の自然環境学の研究者などが、研究に訪れる場所でもある。 現在の[[Shrine Shinto|神社神道]]の神体は「社（やしろ）」であり、神奈備とはいわない。神社神道も本来は日本で自然発生的に生まれた[[Prehistoric religion|原始宗教]]といわれ、[[Nature worship|自然崇拝]]や[[Animism|精霊崇拝]]を内包する[[Ko-Shintō|古神道]]から派生して現在に至る。現在の[[Shinto shrine|神社]]には、主たる{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神}}の[[Kami|尊]]（みこと）とは別に、「自然」という神体が存在するのが常で、神体として[[Shimenawa|注連縄]]が飾られた社とともに、{{ill|Keidai|ja|境内}}の内外に神木や霊石や鎮守の森の湖沼や滝などの神体が存在する。古い神社では、[[Shinto architecture|拝殿]]や[[Shinto architecture|本殿]]もなく、自然の神奈備そのものを祭神として[[Festival|祀]]るところもある。 == 『出雲国風土記』におけるカンナビ山 == [[File:Mount_Obune,_enkei-1.jpg|right|thumb|250x250px|Jinnai Hiyama (Mt. Ofunayama), Tatemae-gun]] 『{{Ill|Izumo Fudoki|de}}』には「カンナビ」の山が4か所記載される。「カンナビ」の漢字表記はさまざま。 ; 意宇郡の神名樋野 : [[Matsue|松江市]]の[[Chausuyama|茶臼山]]に比定。 ; 秋鹿郡の神名火山 : 通説では松江市の[[Asahiyama|朝日山]]に比定される。 ; 楯縫郡の神名樋山 : [[Izumo, Shimane|出雲市]]の大船山に比定。 ; 出雲郡の神名火山 : 出雲市の仏経山に比定。 == 脚注 == == 関連事項 == * [[Age of the Gods|神代]]・上代 * [[Ko-Shintō|古神道]] * [[Himorogi|神籬]]・[[Iwakura (Yorishiro)|磐座]] * [[Shintai|神体]]・[[Yorishiro|依り代]] * 鎮守の森・[[Shinboku|神木]] * [[Sacred mountains|神体山]]・[[Mountain worship|山岳信仰]] * [[Kannagi (Shinto)|かんなぎ]] * 神道の緑性 * 三輪山 [[Category:Forests of Japan]] [[Category:Animism]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

Ofunayama), Tatemae-gun]] 『{{Ill|Izumo Fudoki|de}}』には「カンナビ」の山が4か所記載される。「カンナビ」の漢字表記はさまざま。 ; 意宇郡の神名樋野 : [[Matsue|松江市]]の[[Chausuyama|茶臼山]]に比定。 ; 秋鹿郡の神名火山 : 通説では松江市の[[Asahiyama|朝日山]]に比定される。 ; 楯縫郡の神名樋山 : [[Izumo, Shimane|出雲市]]の大船山に比定。 ; 出雲郡の神名火山 : 出雲市の仏経山に比定。 == 脚注 == == 関連事項 == * [[Age of the Gods|神代]]・上代 * [[Ko-Shintō|古神道]] * [[Himorogi|神籬]]・[[Iwakura (Yorishiro)|磐座]] * [[Shintai|神体]]・[[Yorishiro|依り代]] * 鎮守の森・[[Shinboku|神木]] * [[Sacred mountains|神体山]]・[[Mountain worship|山岳信仰]] * [[Kannagi (Shinto)|かんなぎ]] * 神道の緑性 * 三輪山 [[Category:Forests of Japan]] [[Category:Animism]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

[[Yamato Province|Yamato Country]] [[Ichinomiya|Ichinomiya]], [[Ōmiwa Shrine|Okami Shrine]], Kannabi ([[Sacred mountains|Kintai-san]]). Ookami Shrine has a mountain as its sacred body and does not have a [[Shinto architecture|honden]].]] '''Kannabi''' '''神奈備'''（かむなび・かんなび・かみなび） refers to a region in [[Shinto|shinto]] that hosts a mitsumashiro or [[Yorishiro|yorishiro]] (yorishiro) in which the divine spirit ([[Kami|Kami]] or [[Mitama|Goryo]]) resides. Or, the natural environment as [[Shintai|Kamishiro]] (shintai). In the Manyoshu, there are seven occurrences (22 poems, 23 examples). [[Shintai]] is a mountain or forest where a deity "sits" or "dwells in seclusion," a forest that serves as a [[Himorogi|shinzoku]] or [[Iwakura (Yorishiro)|ganza]], a [[Shinboku|shinboku]] or [[Sacred mountains|shintai]], or a [[Sacred mountains|shintai]] or [[Sacred mountain]][[富士山|霊峰富士]]など。[[山岳信仰]]を参照 as well as sacred areas with distinctive rocks ({{ill|Married Couple Rock|ja|夫婦岩}}) and waterfalls ([[Nachi Falls|Nachi Falls]]). It is also a generic term for a roughly woven area and a rock throne. There is also "Kannabino," where there are no forests or rocks to serve as a shrine. == 概要 == 「カンナビ」は「神並び」の「カンナラビ」が「カンナビ」となったとする説や、「ナビ」は「隠れる」を意味し「神が隠れ籠れる」場所とする説がある。もっとも後者は、上代特殊仮名遣いの発見により否定されている。 神奈備は[[Animism|アニミズム]]でもあり、自然への感謝や畏敬や畏怖の体現であるが、神の住まう{{Ill|Shin'iki|it}}や、{{ill|Eternal world|ja|常世}}（とこよ）と{{ill|This world|ja|現世|de|Diesseits|bs|Dunjaluk}}（うつしよ）の端境、または、その常世と現世をわかつ[[Bai sema|結界]]や、禁足地なども意味する。 自然を手付かずに残す事例として、[[Natural environment|自然環境]]の[[Environmental movement|保護]]の観点からも重視され、里山やその周囲の文化として貴重であり、固有の土壌細菌の発見が新薬の開発のきっかけとなることがあるほか、世界中の自然環境学の研究者などが、研究に訪れる場所でもある。 現在の[[Shrine Shinto|神社神道]]の神体は「社（やしろ）」であり、神奈備とはいわない。神社神道も本来は日本で自然発生的に生まれた[[Prehistoric religion|原始宗教]]といわれ、[[Nature worship|自然崇拝]]や[[Animism|精霊崇拝]]を内包する[[Ko-Shintō|古神道]]から派生して現在に至る。現在の[[Shinto shrine|神社]]には、主たる{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神}}の[[Kami|尊]]（みこと）とは別に、「自然」という神体が存在するのが常で、神体として[[Shimenawa|注連縄]]が飾られた社とともに、{{ill|Keidai|ja|境内}}の内外に神木や霊石や鎮守の森の湖沼や滝などの神体が存在する。古い神社では、[[Shinto architecture|拝殿]]や[[Shinto architecture|本殿]]もなく、自然の神奈備そのものを祭神として[[Festival|祀]]るところもある。 == 『出雲国風土記』におけるカンナビ山 == [[File:Mount_Obune,_enkei-1.jpg|right|thumb|250x250px|Jinnai Hiyama (Mt. Ofunayama), Tatemae-gun]] 『{{Ill|Izumo Fudoki|de}}』には「カンナビ」の山が4か所記載される。「カンナビ」の漢字表記はさまざま。 ; 意宇郡の神名樋野 : [[Matsue|松江市]]の[[Chausuyama|茶臼山]]に比定。 ; 秋鹿郡の神名火山 : 通説では松江市の[[Asahiyama|朝日山]]に比定される。 ; 楯縫郡の神名樋山 : [[Izumo, Shimane|出雲市]]の大船山に比定。 ; 出雲郡の神名火山 : 出雲市の仏経山に比定。 == 脚注 == == 関連事項 == * [[Age of the Gods|神代]]・上代 * [[Ko-Shintō|古神道]] * [[Himorogi|神籬]]・[[Iwakura (Yorishiro)|磐座]] * [[Shintai|神体]]・[[Yorishiro|依り代]] * 鎮守の森・[[Shinboku|神木]] * [[Sacred mountains|神体山]]・[[Mountain worship|山岳信仰]] * [[Kannagi (Shinto)|かんなぎ]] * 神道の緑性 * 三輪山 [[Category:Forests of Japan]] [[Category:Animism]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

[[Yamato Province|Yamato Country]] [[Ichinomiya|Ichinomiya]], [[Ōmiwa Shrine|Okami Shrine]], Kannabi ([[Sacred mountains|Kintai-san]]). Ookami Shrine has a mountain as its sacred body and does not have a [[Shinto architecture|honden]].]] {{Nihongo|Kannabi|神奈備|Kamunabi / Kannabi / Kaminabi}} refers to a region in [[Shinto|shinto]] that hosts a mitsumashiro or [[Yorishiro|yorishiro]] (yorishiro) in which the divine spirit ([[Kami|Kami]] or [[Mitama|Goryo]]) resides. Or, the natural environment as [[Shintai|Kamishiro]] (shintai). In the Manyoshu, there are seven occurrences (22 poems, 23 examples). [[Shintai]] is a mountain or forest where a deity "sits" or "dwells in seclusion," a forest that serves as a [[Himorogi|shinzoku]] or [[Iwakura (Yorishiro)|ganza]], a [[Shinboku|shinboku]] or [[Sacred mountains|shintai]], or a [[Sacred mountains|shintai]] or [[Sacred mountain]][[富士山|霊峰富士]]など。[[山岳信仰]]を参照 as well as sacred areas with distinctive rocks ({{ill|Married Couple Rock|ja|夫婦岩}}) and waterfalls ([[Nachi Falls|Nachi Falls]]). It is also a generic term for a roughly woven area and a rock throne. There is also "Kannabino," where there are no forests or rocks to serve as a shrine. == Outline == There is a theory that the word "kannabi" is derived from "kannarabi", which means "god alignment", or that "nabi" means "hiding" and is a place where "gods hide and bask". The latter, however, has been denied by the discovery of the Kamisai special kana script. 神奈備は[[Animism|アニミズム]]でもあり、自然への感謝や畏敬や畏怖の体現であるが、神の住まう{{Ill|Shin'iki|it}}や、{{ill|Eternal world|ja|常世}}（とこよ）と{{ill|This world|ja|現世|de|Diesseits|bs|Dunjaluk}}（うつしよ）の端境、または、その常世と現世をわかつ[[Bai sema|結界]]や、禁足地なども意味する。 自然を手付かずに残す事例として、[[Natural environment|自然環境]]の[[Environmental movement|保護]]の観点からも重視され、里山やその周囲の文化として貴重であり、固有の土壌細菌の発見が新薬の開発のきっかけとなることがあるほか、世界中の自然環境学の研究者などが、研究に訪れる場所でもある。 現在の[[Shrine Shinto|神社神道]]の神体は「社（やしろ）」であり、神奈備とはいわない。神社神道も本来は日本で自然発生的に生まれた[[Prehistoric religion|原始宗教]]といわれ、[[Nature worship|自然崇拝]]や[[Animism|精霊崇拝]]を内包する[[Ko-Shintō|古神道]]から派生して現在に至る。現在の[[Shinto shrine|神社]]には、主たる{{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神}}の[[Kami|尊]]（みこと）とは別に、「自然」という神体が存在するのが常で、神体として[[Shimenawa|注連縄]]が飾られた社とともに、{{ill|Keidai|ja|境内}}の内外に神木や霊石や鎮守の森の湖沼や滝などの神体が存在する。古い神社では、[[Shinto architecture|拝殿]]や[[Shinto architecture|本殿]]もなく、自然の神奈備そのものを祭神として[[Festival|祀]]るところもある。 == 『出雲国風土記』におけるカンナビ山 == [[File:Mount_Obune,_enkei-1.jpg|right|thumb|250x250px|Jinnai Hiyama (Mt. Ofunayama), Tatemae-gun]] 『{{Ill|Izumo Fudoki|de}}』には「カンナビ」の山が4か所記載される。「カンナビ」の漢字表記はさまざま。 ; 意宇郡の神名樋野 : [[Matsue|松江市]]の[[Chausuyama|茶臼山]]に比定。 ; 秋鹿郡の神名火山 : 通説では松江市の[[Asahiyama|朝日山]]に比定される。 ; 楯縫郡の神名樋山 : [[Izumo, Shimane|出雲市]]の大船山に比定。 ; 出雲郡の神名火山 : 出雲市の仏経山に比定。 == 脚注 == == 関連事項 == * [[Age of the Gods|神代]]・上代 * [[Ko-Shintō|古神道]] * [[Himorogi|神籬]]・[[Iwakura (Yorishiro)|磐座]] * [[Shintai|神体]]・[[Yorishiro|依り代]] * 鎮守の森・[[Shinboku|神木]] * [[Sacred mountains|神体山]]・[[Mountain worship|山岳信仰]] * [[Kannagi (Shinto)|かんなぎ]] * 神道の緑性 * 三輪山 [[Category:Forests of Japan]] [[Category:Animism]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

[[Yamato Province|Yamato Country]] [[Ichinomiya|Ichinomiya]], [[Ōmiwa Shrine|Okami Shrine]], Kannabi ([[Sacred mountains|Kintai-san]]). [[Shintai]] is a mountain or forest where a deity "sits" or "dwells in seclusion," a forest that serves as a [[Himorogi|shinzoku]] or [[Iwakura (Yorishiro)|ganza]], a [[Shinboku|shinboku]] or [[Sacred mountains|shintai]], or a [[Sacred mountains|shintai]] or [[Sacred mountain]][[富士山|霊峰富士]]など。[[山岳信仰]]を参照 as well as sacred areas with distinctive rocks ({{ill|Married Couple Rock|ja|夫婦岩}}) and waterfalls ([[Nachi Falls|Nachi Falls]]). It is also a generic term for a roughly woven area and a rock throne. There is also "Kannabino," where there are no forests or rocks to serve as a shrine. == Outline == There is a theory that the word "kannabi" is derived from "kannarabi", which means "god alignment", or that "nabi" means "hiding" and is a place where "gods hide and bask". The latter, however, has been denied by the discovery of the Kamisai special kana script. 神奈備は[[Animism|アニミズム]]でもあり、自然への感謝や畏敬や畏怖の体現であるが、神の住まう{{Ill|Shin'iki|it}}や、{{ill|Eternal world|ja|常世}}（とこよ）と{{ill|This world|ja|現世|de|Diesseits|bs|Dunjaluk}}（うつしよ）の端境、または、その常世と現世をわかつ[[Bai sema|結界]]や、禁足地なども意味する。 As an example of leaving nature untouched, it is emphasized from the perspective of [[environmental movement|protection]] of the [[Natural environment|natural environment]]. It is valuable as a satoyama and its surrounding culture, and the discovery of endemic soil bacteria has sometimes led to the development of new drugs, and it is a place where natural environmental researchers and others from around the world visit for research. The Shinto body of today's [[Shrine Shinto|Shrine Shinto]] is a "shrine," not a kannabi-sha. Shrine Shinto is also said to be a [[Prehistoric religion|primitive religion]] that originally arose spontaneously in Japan, and was derived from [[Ko-Shintō|Kojindo]], which includes [[Nature worship|nature worship]] and [[animism|spirit worship]]. In today's [[Shinto shrine]]s, there is usually a deity called "nature" in addition to the [[Kami|Mikoto]] of the main {{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神}}, and along with the shrine decorated with [[Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]s as the deity, there is also a shrine called {{ill|Keidai|ja|境内}}. inside and outside the shrine, there are sacred trees, sacred stones, and sacred bodies such as lakes, marshes, and waterfalls in the town's forests. In some older shrines, there is no [[Shinto architecture|worship hall]] or [[Shinto architecture|honden]], and the natural kannabis themselves are [[festival|enshrine]]ed as deities. == Kannabi in Izumo Fudoki == [[File:Mount_Obune,_enkei-1.jpg|right|thumb|250x250px|Jinnai Hiyama (Mt. Ofunayama), Tatemae-gun]] The {{Ill|Izumo Fudoki|de}} (Izumo Province Fudoki) mentions four "Kannabi" mountains. The kanji for "Kannabi" varies. ; 意宇郡の神名樋野 : [[Matsue|松江市]]の[[Chausuyama|茶臼山]]に比定。 ; 秋鹿郡の神名火山 : 通説では松江市の[[Asahiyama|朝日山]]に比定される。 ; 楯縫郡の神名樋山 : [[Izumo, Shimane|出雲市]]の大船山に比定。 ; 出雲郡の神名火山 : 出雲市の仏経山に比定。 == 脚注 == == See Also == * [[Age of the Gods]] * [[Ko-Shintō]] * [[Himorogi]]・[[Iwakura (Yorishiro)]] * [[Shintai]]・[[Yorishiro]] * [[Shinboku]] * [[Sacred mountains]]・[[Mountain worship]] * [[Kannagi (Shinto)]] [[Category:Forests of Japan]] [[Category:Animism]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

In some older shrines, there is no [[Shinto architecture|worship hall]] or [[Shinto architecture|honden]], and the natural kannabis themselves are [[festival|enshrine]]ed as deities. == Kannabi in Izumo Fudoki == [[File:Mount_Obune,_enkei-1.jpg|right|thumb|250x250px|Jinnai Hiyama (Mt. Ofunayama), Tatemae-gun]] The {{Ill|Izumo Fudoki|de}} (Izumo Province Fudoki) mentions four "Kannabi" mountains. Or, the natural environment as [[Shintai|Kamishiro]] (shintai). In the Manyoshu, there are seven occurrences (22 poems, 23 examples). [[Shintai]] is a mountain or forest where a deity "sits" or "dwells in seclusion," a forest that serves as a [[Himorogi|shinzoku]] or [[Iwakura (Yorishiro)|ganza]], a [[Shinboku|shinboku]] or [[Sacred mountains|shintai]], or a [[Sacred mountains|shintai]] or [[Sacred mountain]][[富士山|霊峰富士]]など。[[山岳信仰]]を参照 as well as sacred areas with distinctive rocks ({{ill|Meoto Iwa|ja|夫婦岩}}) and waterfalls ([[Nachi Falls|Nachi Falls]]). Diesseits|bs|Dunjaluk}} (Utsushi-yo), or the [[Bai sema|boundary]] that separates this world from the eternal world, or the forbidden land. In today's [[Shinto shrine]]s, there is usually a deity called "nature" in addition to the [[Kami|Mikoto]] of the main {{ill|Saijin|ja|祭神}}, and along with the shrine decorated with [[Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]s as the deity, there is also a shrine called {{ill|Keidai|ja|境内}}. inside and outside the shrine, there are sacred trees, sacred stones, and sacred bodies such as lakes, marshes, and waterfalls in the town's forests. {{Short description|Sacred locations in Shinto}} [[File:Miwayama201805.jpg|thumb|300x300px|[[Mount Miwa]]

[[Yamato Province|Yamato Country]] [[Ichinomiya]], [[Ōmiwa Shrine|Okami Shrine]], Kannabi ([[Sacred mountains|Kintai-san]]). Ookami Shrine has a mountain as its sacred body and does not have a [[Shinto architecture|honden]].]] {{Nihongo|Kannabi|神奈備|Kamunabi / Kannabi / Kaminabi}} refers to a region in [[shinto]] that hosts a mitsumashiro or [[yorishiro]] (yorishiro) in which the divine spirit ([[Kami]] or [[Mitama|Goryo]]) resides. Or, the natural environment as [[Shintai|Kamishiro]] (shintai). In the Manyoshu, there are seven occurrences (22 poems, 23 examples). [[Shintai]] is a mountain or forest where a deity "sits" or "dwells in seclusion," a forest that serves as a [[Himorogi|shinzoku]] or [[Iwakura (Yorishiro)|ganza]], a [[shinboku]] or [[Sacred mountains|shintai]], or a [[Sacred mountains|shintai]] or [[Sacred mountain]][[富士山|霊峰富士]]など。[[山岳信仰]]を参照 as well as sacred areas with distinctive rocks ([[Meoto Iwa]]) and waterfalls ([[Nachi Falls]]). It is also a generic term for a roughly woven area and a rock throne. There is also "Kannabino," where there are no forests or rocks to serve as a shrine. == Outline == The origin of the word "kannabi" is a subject of debate, with some theories suggesting it stems from "Kannarabi (神並び)" meaning "god alignment," while others propose "nabi" means "hiding" and refers to a place where the gods retreat. This latter theory has been discredited by the discovery of the Kamisai special kana script. Kannabi embodies the principles of [[animism]], including [[gratitude]], [[respect]], and [[awe]] for [[nature]]. It also serves as the dividing line between the world of the gods ({{Ill|Shin'iki|it}} or {{ill|Tokoyo (eternal night)|lt=Tokoyo|ja|常世}}) and the {{ill|This world|lt=this world|ja|現世|de|Diesseits|bs|Dunjaluk}}. Emphasizing the preservation of nature, kannabi is seen as a valuable aspect of satoyama and its surrounding culture. The discovery of endemic soil bacteria in kannabi has sometimes led to the development of new drugs, making it a popular destination for natural environment researchers from around the world. Contrary to popular belief, today's "[[Shrine Shinto]]" is not kannabi-sha but is instead referred to as "shrine." Shrine Shinto is considered a [[Prehistoric religion|primitive religion]] that arose spontaneously in Japan and evolved from [[Ko-Shintō]], which encompasses [[nature worship]] and [[animism|spirit worship]]. In contemporary [[Shinto shrine]]s, there is often a deity known as "nature" in addition to the main deity. The shrines are adorned with [[Shimenawa|Shimenawas]], and there may also be a {{ill|Keidai|ja|境内}} shrine within. The surrounding areas may feature sacred trees, stones, and natural elements such as lakes, marshes, and waterfalls in the town's forests, which are considered sacred. Some older shrines lack [[Haiden (Shinto)|worship halls]] or [[honden|hondens]] and instead enshrine the natural kannabis as deities. == Kannabi in Izumo Fudoki == [[File:Mount_Obune,_enkei-1.jpg|right|thumb|250x250px|Jinnai Hiyama (Mt. Ofunayama), Tatemae-gun]] The {{Ill|Izumo Fudoki|de}} (Izumo Province Fudoki) mentions four "Kannabi" mountains. The kanji for "Kannabi" varies. ; Shinmei-Hino in Iewu-gun. : [[Matsue|Matsue City]], compared to [[Chausuyama (Matsue)|Chausuyama]]. ; Akiga County's Shinmei Volcano : According to the conventional wisdom, it is compared to [[Mount Asahi (Matsue)|Asahiyama]] in Matsue City. ; Jinnai Hiyama in Tatemae-gun : [[Izumo, Shimane | Izumo City]] Mt. Taisen. ; Shinmei Volcano, Izumo County : Izumo City, Izumo Prefecture, Japan. == References == == See Also == * [[Age of the Gods]] * [[Ko-Shintō]] * [[Himorogi]]・[[Iwakura (Yorishiro)]] * [[Shintai]]・[[Yorishiro]] * [[Shinboku]] * [[Sacred mountains]]・[[Mountain worship]] * [[Kannagi (Shinto)]] * [[Chinju no Mori]] [[Category:Forests of Japan]] [[Category:Animism]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

The discovery of endemic soil bacteria in kannabi has sometimes led to the development of new drugs, making it a popular destination for natural environment researchers from around the world. Contrary to popular belief, today's "[[Shrine Shinto]]" is not kannabi-sha but is instead referred to as "shrine." Shrine Shinto is considered a [[Prehistoric religion|primitive religion]] that arose spontaneously in Japan and evolved from [[Ko-Shintō]], which encompasses [[nature worship]] and [[animism|spirit worship]]. In contemporary [[Shinto shrine]]s, there is often a deity known as "nature" in addition to the main deity. The shrines are adorned with [[Shimenawa|Shimenawas]], and there may also be a {{ill|Keidai|ja|境内}} shrine within. The surrounding areas may feature sacred trees, stones, and natural elements such as lakes, marshes, and waterfalls in the town's forests, which are considered sacred. Some older shrines lack [[Haiden (Shinto)|worship halls]] or [[honden|hondens]] and instead enshrine the natural kannabis as deities. == References == == See Also == * [[Age of the Gods]] * [[Ko-Shintō]] * [[Himorogi]]・[[Iwakura (Yorishiro)]] * [[Shintai]]・[[Yorishiro]] * [[Shinboku]] * [[Sacred mountains]]・[[Mountain worship]] * [[Kannagi (Shinto)]] * [[Chinju no Mori]] [[Category:Forests of Japan]] [[Category:Animism]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

revision.comment: Just took bibliography for one Citation by Tamamura

revision.text:
{{Short description|Sacred locations in Shinto}}
[[File:Miwayama201805.jpg|thumb|300x300px|[[Ōmiwa Shrine|Omiwa Shrine]] has [[Mount Miwa]] as its [[Shintai]] and does not have a [[Shinto architecture|honden]].<ref name=":2">{{cite book |last=Tamura |first=Yoshiro |title=Japanese Buddhism - A Cultural History |publisher=Kosei Publishing Company |year=2000 |isbn=4-333-01684-3 |location=Tokyo |pages=21 |chapter=The Birth of the Japanese Nation}}</ref>]] {{Nihongo|Kannabi|神奈備|Kamunabi / Kannabi / Kaminabi}} refers to a region in [[shinto]] that is a [[Shintai]] itself, or hosts a kami.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp//eos/detail/ |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> They are generally either [[Mountain worship|mountains]] or [[Chinju no Mori|forests]].<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1">[[富士山|霊峰富士]]など。[[山岳信仰]]を参照</ref>
They may be host to [[Shinboku]] (sacred trees), or [[Iwakura rocks]]<ref name=":1" />
Although natural locations, Kannabi will still have [[Shimenawa]], [[Torii]], and [[Sandō]] marking the path towards them.{{Citation needed|date=March 2023}}
[[Nachi Falls]] is considered a Kannabi<ref name="nachi2">{{cite web |last=Kamizaka |first=Jirō |title=Hiryū Gongen |url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html |access-date=28 March 2010 |publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau |language=ja}}</ref>, as is [[Mount Miwa]].<ref name=":2" />
Shrines dedicated to Kannabi often lack [[Haiden (Shinto)|worship halls]] or [[honden|hondens]] and instead enshrine the natural kannabis as deities. [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] is one such example.{{Citation needed|date=March 2023}}
== References == <references />
== See Also ==
* [[Age of the Gods]] * [[Ko-Shintō]] * [[Himorogi]]・[[Iwakura (Yorishiro)]] * [[Shintai]]・[[Yorishiro]] * [[Shinboku]] * [[Sacred mountains]]・[[Mountain worship]] * [[Kannagi (Shinto)]] * [[Chinju no Mori]]
== Bibliography ==
*

They may be host to [[Shinboku]] (sacred trees), or [[Iwakura rocks]]<ref name=":1" />
Although natural locations, Kannabi will still have [[Shimenawa]], [[Torii]], and [[Sandō]] marking the path towards them.{{Citation needed|date=March 2023}}
[[Nachi Falls]] is considered a Kannabi<ref name="nachi2">{{cite web |last=Kamizaka |first=Jirō |title=Hiryū Gongen |url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html |access-date=28 March 2010 |publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau |language=ja}}</ref>, as is [[Mount Miwa]].<ref name=":2" />
Shrines dedicated to Kannabi often lack [[Haiden (Shinto)|worship halls]] or [[honden|hondens]] and instead enshrine the natural kannabis as deities. [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] is one such example.<ref name=":2" />

{{Short description|Sacred locations in Shinto}}
[[File:Miwayama201805.jpg|thumb|300x300px|[[Ōmiwa Shrine|Omiwa Shrine]] has [[Mount Miwa]] as its [[Shintai]] and does not have a [[Shinto architecture|honden]].<ref name="tamura-2000a">{{cite book |last=Tamura |first=Yoshiro |title=Japanese Buddhism - A Cultural History |publisher=Kosei Publishing Company |year=2000 |isbn=4-333-01684-3 |location=Tokyo |pages=21 |chapter=The Birth of the Japanese Nation}}</ref>]] {{Nihongo|'''''Kannabi'''''|神奈備}}, also '''''kaminabi''''' or '''''kamunabi''''', refers to a region in [[Shinto]] that is a ''[[shintai]]'' (repositories in which ''[[kami]]'' reside) itself, or hosts a ''kami''.<ref name="https-9690a">https://archive.today/20230407173533/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9690</ref> They are generally either [[Mountain worship|mountains]] or [[Chinju no Mori|forests]].<ref name="https-9690a" /><ref name="霊峰富士なと-2">[[富士山|霊峰富士]]など。[[山岳信仰]]を参照</ref> [[Nachi Falls]] is considered a ''kannabi'',<ref name="nachi2">{{cite web |last=Kamizaka |first=Jirō |title=Hiryū Gongen |url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html |access-date=28 March 2010 |publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau |language=ja}}</ref> as is [[Mount Miwa]].<ref name="tamura-2000a" />
== Overview == They may be host to ''[[shinboku]]'' (sacred trees), or [[Iwakura rocks]]<ref name="霊峰富士なと-2" /> They may have ''[[shimenawa]]'', ''[[torii]]'', and ''[[sandō]]'' marking the path towards them.{{Citation needed|date=March 2023}}
Shrines dedicated to ''kannabi'' often lack a ''[[honden]]'' or [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']], and instead enshrine the natural ''kannabi'' as deities. [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] is one such example.<ref name="tamura-2000a" /> [[Kanasana Shrine]] also has its mountain be its Shintai<ref>{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Age of the Gods]] * [[Ko-Shintō]] * [[Himorogi]]・[[Iwakura (Yorishiro)]] * [[Shintai]]・[[Yorishiro]] * [[Shinboku]] * [[Sacred mountains]]・[[Mountain worship]] * [[Kannagi (Shinto)|Kannagi]] which has a similar etymology * [[Chinju no Mori]] == References == <references />

[[File:Miwayama201805.jpg|thumb|300x300px|[[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Omiwa Shrine]] has [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]] as its [[Shintai]] and does not have a [[:en:Shinto architecture|honden]].<ref name="tamura-2000a">{{cite book |last=Tamura |first=Yoshiro |title=Japanese Buddhism - A Cultural History |publisher=Kosei Publishing Company |year=2000 |isbn=4-333-01684-3 |location=Tokyo |pages=21 |chapter=The Birth of the Japanese Nation}}</ref>]] {{Nihongo|'''''Kannabi'''''|神奈備}}, also '''''kaminabi''''' or '''''kamunabi''''', refers to a region in [[Shinto]] that is a ''[[shintai]]'' (repositories in which ''[[kami]]'' reside) itself, or hosts a ''kami''.<ref name="https-9690a">https://archive.today/20230407173533/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9690</ref> They are generally either [[:en:Mountain worship|mountains]] or [[Chinju no Mori|forests]].<ref name="https-9690a" /><ref name="霊峰富士なと-2">[[:en:富士山|霊峰富士]]など。[[:en:山岳信仰|山岳信仰]]を参照</ref> [[Nachi Falls]] is considered a ''kannabi'',<ref name="nachi2">{{cite web |last=Kamizaka |first=Jirō |title=Hiryū Gongen |url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html |access-date=28 March 2010 |publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau |language=ja}}</ref> as is [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]].<ref name="tamura-2000a" />
== Overview == They may be host to ''[[shinboku]]'' (sacred trees), or [[Iwakura rock|Iwakura rocks]]<ref name="霊峰富士なと-2" /> They may have ''[[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]'', ''[[torii]]'', and ''[[:en:Sandō|Sandō]]'' marking the path towards them.{{Citation needed|date=March 2023}}
Shrines dedicated to ''kannabi'' often lack a ''[[honden]]'' or [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']], and instead enshrine the natural ''kannabi'' as deities. [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] is one such example.<ref name="tamura-2000a" /> [[Kanasana Shrine]] also has its mountain be its Shintai<ref>{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Age of the Gods]] * [[Ko-Shintō]] * [[Himorogi]]・[[Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Yorishiro)]] * [[Shintai]]・[[:en:Yorishiro|Yorishiro]] * [[Shinboku]] * [[:en:Sacred mountains|Sacred mountains]]・[[:en:Mountain worship|Mountain worship]] * [[Kannagi (Shinto)|Kannagi]] which has a similar etymology * [[Chinju no Mori]] == References == <references />

[[Category:Forests of Japan]] [[Category:Animism]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:pages with unreviewed translations]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto]]
}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kannabi]][[fa:کامونابی]][[fr:Kannabi]][[ja:神奈備]][[simple:Kannabi]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11588874]] Wikidata name: kamunabi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11588874]] Wikidata name: kamunabi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Sacred locations in Shinto}}
[[File:Miwayama201805.jpg|thumb|300x300px|[[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Omiwa Shrine]] has [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]] as its [[Shintai]] and does not have a [[:en:Shinto architecture|honden]].<ref name="tamura-2000a">{{cite book |last=Tamura |first=Yoshiro |title=Japanese Buddhism - A Cultural History |publisher=Kosei Publishing Company |year=2000 |isbn=4-333-01684-3 |location=Tokyo |pages=21 |chapter=The Birth of the Japanese Nation}}</ref>]] {{Nihongo|'''''Kannabi'''''|神奈備}}, also '''''kaminabi''''' or '''''kamunabi''''', refers to a region in [[Shinto]] that is a ''[[shintai]]'' (repositories in which ''[[kami]]'' reside) itself, or hosts a ''kami''.<ref name="https-9690a">https://archive.today/20230407173533/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9690</ref> They are generally either [[:en:Mountain worship|mountains]] or [[Chinju no Mori|forests]].<ref name="https-9690a" /><ref name="霊峰富士なと-2">[[:en:富士山|霊峰富士]]など。[[:en:山岳信仰|山岳信仰]]を参照</ref> [[Nachi Falls]] is considered a ''kannabi'',<ref name="nachi2">{{cite web |last=Kamizaka |first=Jirō |title=Hiryū Gongen |url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html |access-date=28 March 2010 |publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau |language=ja}}</ref> as is [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]].<ref name="tamura-2000a" />
== Overview == They may be host to ''[[shinboku]]'' (sacred trees), or [[Iwakura rock|Iwakura rocks]]<ref name="霊峰富士なと-2" /> They may have ''[[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]'', ''[[torii]]'', and ''[[:en:Sandō|Sandō]]'' marking the path towards them.{{Citation needed|date=March 2023}}
Shrines dedicated to ''kannabi'' often lack a ''[[honden]]'' or [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']], and instead enshrine the natural ''kannabi'' as deities. [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] is one such example.<ref name="tamura-2000a" /> [[Kanasana Shrine]] also has its mountain be its Shintai<ref>{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Age of the Gods]] * [[Ko-Shintō]] * [[Himorogi]]・[[Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Yorishiro)]] * [[Shintai]]・[[:en:Yorishiro|Yorishiro]] * [[Shinboku]] * [[:en:Sacred mountains|Sacred mountains]]・[[:en:Mountain worship|Mountain worship]] * [[Kannagi (Shinto)|Kannagi]] which has a similar etymology * [[Chinju no Mori]] == References == <references />

Shrines dedicated to ''kannabi'' often lack a ''[[honden]]'' or [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']], and instead enshrine the natural ''kannabi'' as deities. [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] is one such example.<ref name="tamura-2000a" /> [[Kanasana Shrine]] also has its mountain be its Shintai<ref>{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>

[[File:Miwayama201805.jpg|thumb|300x300px|[[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Omiwa Shrine]] has [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]] as its [[Shintai]] and does not have a [[:en:Shinto architecture|honden]].<ref name="tamura-2000a">{{cite book |last=Tamura |first=Yoshiro |title=Japanese Buddhism - A Cultural History |publisher=Kosei Publishing Company |year=2000 |isbn=4-333-01684-3 |location=Tokyo |pages=21 |chapter=The Birth of the Japanese Nation}}</ref>]] {{Nihongo|'''''Kannabi'''''|神奈備}}, also '''''kaminabi''''' or '''''kamunabi''''', refers to a region in [[Shinto]] that is a ''[[shintai]]'' (repositories in which ''[[kami]]'' reside) itself, or hosts a ''kami''.<ref name="https-9690a">https://archive.today/20230407173533/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9690</ref> They are generally either [[:en:Mountain worship|mountains]] or [[Chinju no Mori|forests]].<ref name="https-9690a" /><ref name="霊峰富士なと-2">[[:en:富士山|霊峰富士]]など。[[:en:山岳信仰|山岳信仰]]を参照</ref> [[Nachi Falls]] is considered a ''kannabi'',<ref name="nachi2">{{cite web |last=Kamizaka |first=Jirō |title=Hiryū Gongen |url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html |access-date=28 March 2010 |publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau |language=ja}}</ref> as is [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]].<ref name="tamura-2000a" />
== Overview == They may be host to ''[[shinboku]]'' (sacred trees), or [[Iwakura rock|Iwakura rocks]]<ref name="霊峰富士なと-2" /> They may have ''[[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]'', ''[[torii]]'', and ''[[:en:Sandō|Sandō]]'' marking the path towards them.{{Citation needed|date=March 2023}}
Shrines dedicated to ''kannabi'' often lack a ''[[honden]]'' or [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']], and instead enshrine the natural ''kannabi'' as deities. [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] is one such example.<ref name="tamura-2000a" /> [[Kanasana Shrine]] also has its mountain be its Shintai<ref>{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Age of the Gods]] * [[Ko-Shintō]] * [[Himorogi]]・[[Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Yorishiro)]] * [[Shintai]]・[[:en:Yorishiro|Yorishiro]] * [[Shinboku]] * [[:en:Sacred mountains|Sacred mountains]]・[[:en:Mountain worship|Mountain worship]] * [[Kannagi (Shinto)|Kannagi]] which has a similar etymology * [[Chinju no Mori]] == References == <references />

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kannabi]][[fa:کامونابی]][[fr:Kannabi]][[ja:神奈備]][[simple:Kannabi]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kannabi]][[fa:کامونابی]][[fr:Kannabi]][[ja:神奈備]][[simple:Kannabi]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kannabi]][[fa:کامونابی]][[fr:Kannabi]][[ja:神奈備]][[simple:Kannabi]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kannabi]][[fa:کامونابی]][[fr:Kannabi]][[ja:神奈備]][[simple:Kannabi]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11588874]] Wikidata name: kamunabi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11588874]] Wikidata name: kamunabi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Sacred locations in Shinto}}
[[File:Miwayama201805.jpg|thumb|300x300px|{{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|WD=Q705542|lt=Omiwa Shrine}} has {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}} as its [[Shintai]] and does not have a {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=honden}}.<ref name="tamura-2000a">{{cite book |last=Tamura |first=Yoshiro |title=Japanese Buddhism - A Cultural History |publisher=Kosei Publishing Company |year=2000 |isbn=4-333-01684-3 |location=Tokyo |pages=21 |chapter=The Birth of the Japanese Nation}}</ref>]] {{Nihongo|'''''Kannabi'''''|神奈備}}, also '''''kaminabi''''' or '''''kamunabi''''', refers to a region in [[Shinto]] that is a ''[[shintai]]'' (repositories in which ''[[kami]]'' reside) itself, or hosts a ''kami''.<ref name="https-9690a">https://archive.today/20230407173533/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9690</ref> They are generally either {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|WD=Q93697439|lt=mountains}} or [[Chinju no Mori|forests]].<ref name="https-9690a" /><ref name="霊峰富士なと-2">[[:en:富士山|霊峰富士]]など。[[:en:山岳信仰|山岳信仰]]を参照</ref> [[Nachi Falls]] is considered a ''kannabi'',<ref name="nachi2">{{cite web |last=Kamizaka |first=Jirō |title=Hiryū Gongen |url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html |access-date=28 March 2010 |publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau |language=ja}}</ref> as is {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}}.<ref name="tamura-2000a" />
== Overview == They may be host to ''[[shinboku]]'' (sacred trees), or [[Iwakura rock|Iwakura rocks]]<ref name="霊峰富士なと-2" /> They may have ''{{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|WD=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa}}'', ''[[torii]]'', and ''{{ill|Sandō|en|Sandō|WD=Q667783|lt=Sandō}}'' marking the path towards them.{{Citation needed|date=March 2023}}
Shrines dedicated to ''kannabi'' often lack a ''[[honden]]'' or [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']], and instead enshrine the natural ''kannabi'' as deities. {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|WD=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine}} is one such example.<ref name="tamura-2000a" /> [[Kanasana Shrine]] also has its mountain be its Shintai<ref>{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Age of the Gods]] * [[Ko-Shintō]] * [[Himorogi]]・[[Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Yorishiro)]] * [[Shintai]]・{{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|WD=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro}} * [[Shinboku]] * {{ill|Sacred mountains|en|Sacred mountains|WD=Q1595289|lt=Sacred mountains}}・{{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|WD=Q93697439|lt=Mountain worship}} * [[Kannagi (Shinto)|Kannagi]] which has a similar etymology * [[Chinju no Mori]] == References == <references />
<!--this was an inappropriate and erroneous transclusion meant to be a bottom bar thing I think but not including-->
[[en:Kannabi]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:کامونابی]] [[fr:Kannabi]] [[ja:神奈備]] [[simple:Kannabi]] {{wikidata link|Q11588874}} [[Category:Animism]] [[Category:Forests of Japan]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:pages with unreviewed translations]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kannabi]][[fa:کامونابی]][[fr:Kannabi]][[ja:神奈備]][[simple:Kannabi]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kannabi]][[fa:کامونابی]][[fr:Kannabi]][[ja:神奈備]][[simple:Kannabi]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kannabi]][[fa:کامونابی]][[fr:Kannabi]][[ja:神奈備]][[simple:Kannabi]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kannabi]][[fa:کامونابی]][[fr:Kannabi]][[ja:神奈備]][[simple:Kannabi]]
{{Short description|Sacred locations in Shinto}}
[[File:Miwayama201805.jpg|thumb|300x300px|{{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|WD=Q705542|lt=Omiwa Shrine}} has {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}} as its [[Shintai]] and does not have a {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=honden}}.<ref name="tamura-2000a">{{cite book |last=Tamura |first=Yoshiro |title=Japanese Buddhism - A Cultural History |publisher=Kosei Publishing Company |year=2000 |isbn=4-333-01684-3 |location=Tokyo |pages=21 |chapter=The Birth of the Japanese Nation}}</ref>]] {{Nihongo|'''''Kannabi'''''|神奈備}}, also '''''kaminabi''''' or '''''kamunabi''''', refers to a region in [[Shinto]] that is a ''[[shintai]]'' (repositories in which ''[[kami]]'' reside) itself, or hosts a ''kami''.<ref name="https-9690a">https://archive.today/20230407173533/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9690</ref> They are generally either {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|WD=Q93697439|lt=mountains}} or [[Chinju no Mori|forests]].<ref name="https-9690a" /><ref name="霊峰富士なと-2">[[:en:富士山|霊峰富士]]など。[[:en:山岳信仰|山岳信仰]]を参照</ref> [[Nachi Falls]] is considered a ''kannabi'',<ref name="nachi2">{{cite web |last=Kamizaka |first=Jirō |title=Hiryū Gongen |url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html |access-date=28 March 2010 |publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau |language=ja}}</ref> as is {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}}.<ref name="tamura-2000a" />
== Overview == They may be host to ''[[shinboku]]'' (sacred trees), or [[Iwakura rock|Iwakura rocks]]<ref name="霊峰富士なと-2" /> They may have ''{{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|WD=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa}}'', ''[[torii]]'', and ''{{ill|Sandō|en|Sandō|WD=Q667783|lt=Sandō}}'' marking the path towards them.{{Citation needed|date=March 2023}}
Shrines dedicated to ''kannabi'' often lack a ''[[honden]]'' or [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']], and instead enshrine the natural ''kannabi'' as deities. {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|WD=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine}} is one such example.<ref name="tamura-2000a" /> [[Kanasana Shrine]] also has its mountain be its Shintai<ref>{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Age of the Gods]] * [[Ko-Shintō]] * [[Himorogi]]・[[Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Yorishiro)]] * [[Shintai]]・{{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|WD=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro}} * [[Shinboku]] * {{ill|Sacred mountains|en|Sacred mountains|WD=Q1595289|lt=Sacred mountains}}・{{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|WD=Q93697439|lt=Mountain worship}} * [[Kannagi (Shinto)|Kannagi]] which has a similar etymology * [[Chinju no Mori]] == References == <references />
<!--this was an inappropriate and erroneous transclusion meant to be a bottom bar thing I think but not including-->
[[en:Kannabi]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:کامونابی]] [[fr:Kannabi]] [[ja:神奈備]] [[simple:Kannabi]] {{wikidata link|Q11588874}} [[Category:Animism]] [[Category:Forests of Japan]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:pages with unreviewed translations]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

== Overview == They may be host to ''[[shinboku]]'' (sacred trees), or [[Iwakura rock|Iwakura rocks]]<ref name="霊峰富士なと-2" /> They may have ''{{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|WD=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa|1=Shimenawa}}'', ''[[torii]]'', and ''{{ill|Sandō|en|Sandō|WD=Q667783|lt=Sandō|1=Sandō}}'' marking the path towards them.{{Citation needed|date=March 2023}}
Shrines dedicated to ''kannabi'' often lack a ''[[honden]]'' or [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']], and instead enshrine the natural ''kannabi'' as deities. {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|WD=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}} is one such example.<ref name="tamura-2000a" /> [[Kanasana Shrine]] also has its mountain be its Shintai<ref>{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Age of the Gods]] * [[Ko-Shintō]] * [[Himorogi]]・[[Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Yorishiro)]] * [[Shintai]]・{{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|WD=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro|1=Yorishiro}} * [[Shinboku]] * {{ill|Sacred mountains|en|Sacred mountains|WD=Q1595289|lt=Sacred mountains|1=Sacred mountains}}・{{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|WD=Q93697439|lt=Mountain worship|1=Mountain worship}} * [[Kannagi (Shinto)|Kannagi]] which has a similar etymology * [[Chinju no Mori]] == References == <references />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kannabi]][[fa:کامونابی]][[fr:Kannabi]][[ja:神奈備]][[simple:Kannabi]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kannabi]][[fa:کامونابی]][[fr:Kannabi]][[ja:神奈備]][[simple:Kannabi]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kannabi]][[fa:کامونابی]][[fr:Kannabi]][[ja:神奈備]][[simple:Kannabi]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kannabi]][[fa:کامونابی]][[fr:Kannabi]][[ja:神奈備]][[simple:Kannabi]]
{{Short description|Sacred locations in Shinto}}
[[File:Miwayama201805.jpg|thumb|300x300px|{{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|qid=Q705542|lt=Omiwa Shrine|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}} has {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|qid=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}} as its [[Shintai]] and does not have a {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|qid=Q3080012|lt=honden|1=Shinto architecture}}.<ref name="tamura-2000a">{{cite book |last=Tamura |first=Yoshiro |title=Japanese Buddhism - A Cultural History |publisher=Kosei Publishing Company |year=2000 |isbn=4-333-01684-3 |location=Tokyo |pages=21 |chapter=The Birth of the Japanese Nation}}</ref>]] {{Nihongo|'''''Kannabi'''''|神奈備}}, also '''''kaminabi''''' or '''''kamunabi''''', refers to a region in [[Shinto]] that is a ''[[shintai]]'' (repositories in which ''[[kami]]'' reside) itself, or hosts a ''kami''.<ref name="https-9690a">https://archive.today/20230407173533/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9690</ref> They are generally either {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|qid=Q93697439|lt=mountains|1=Mountain worship}} or [[Chinju no Mori|forests]].<ref name="https-9690a" /><ref name="霊峰富士なと-2">[[:en:富士山|霊峰富士]]など。[[:en:山岳信仰|山岳信仰]]を参照</ref> [[Nachi Falls]] is considered a ''kannabi'',<ref name="nachi2">{{cite web |last=Kamizaka |first=Jirō |title=Hiryū Gongen |url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html |access-date=28 March 2010 |publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau |language=ja}}</ref> as is {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|qid=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}}.<ref name="tamura-2000a" />
== Overview == They may be host to ''[[shinboku]]'' (sacred trees), or [[Iwakura rock|Iwakura rocks]]<ref name="霊峰富士なと-2" /> They may have ''{{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|qid=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa|1=Shimenawa}}'', ''[[torii]]'', and ''{{ill|Sandō|en|Sandō|qid=Q667783|lt=Sandō|1=Sandō}}'' marking the path towards them.{{Citation needed|date=March 2023}}
Shrines dedicated to ''kannabi'' often lack a ''[[honden]]'' or [[Haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']], and instead enshrine the natural ''kannabi'' as deities. {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|qid=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}} is one such example.<ref name="tamura-2000a" /> [[Kanasana Shrine]] also has its mountain be its Shintai<ref>{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Age of the Gods]] * [[Ko-Shintō]] * [[Himorogi]]・[[Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Yorishiro)]] * [[Shintai]]・{{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|qid=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro|1=Yorishiro}} * [[Shinboku]] * {{ill|Sacred mountains|en|Sacred mountains|qid=Q1595289|lt=Sacred mountains|1=Sacred mountains}}・{{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|qid=Q93697439|lt=Mountain worship|1=Mountain worship}} * [[Kannagi (Shinto)|Kannagi]] which has a similar etymology * [[Chinju no Mori]] == References == <references />
